metric_name: Enterprise NT buyer criterion 1: Strategic commitment to NT
metric_value: IT managers require vendors to have demonstrated, public strategic commitment to NT as primary enterprise platform
notes: Factor 1 of 4-criteria buying model derived from IT executive interviews

metric_name: Enterprise NT buyer criterion 2: Product breadth and extensions
metric_value: IT managers require vendors to offer full product breadth from desktop to server plus platform extensions (scalability, availability, management)
notes: Factor 2 of 4-criteria buying model

metric_name: Enterprise NT buyer criterion 3: Integration and deployment services
metric_value: IT managers require vendors with enterprise-experienced professional services organizations for NT deployment in mission-critical environments
notes: Factor 3 of 4-criteria; digital had 5,000 MS support staff including 1,000 MCPs

metric_name: Enterprise NT buyer criterion 4: Financial stability
metric_value: IT managers require financial stability from NT platform vendors for long-term support commitments
notes: Factor 4 of 4-criteria; ironically DEC failed this criterion in 1998 via acquisition

metric_name: NT applications available on Digital Alpha
metric_value: 1,400+ client/server applications on Alpha platform; direct support for 300 PC and NOS applications
notes: Second-key-platform porting status for Alpha after Intel

metric_value: 5,000+ Microsoft support personnel including 1,000 Microsoft Certified Professionals (ramping to 1,700 near-term)
notes: Largest Microsoft professional services organization in world at time of publication

metric_name: Digital NT server ISV application partners
metric_value: 1,400+ NT applications qualified on Alpha platforms including database, business, banking/financial, groupware, Internet, software tools
notes: Application qualification was strategic bottleneck; DEC aggressive in ISV recruitment

metric_value: Continuous computing server for back-end database/application services supporting NT clients; VMS migration path via Affinity program
notes: Positioned to complement not compete with NT; VMS users provided transition tools

metric_value: High-end 64-bit Unix for decision-support, data warehouses; 32-bit NT not expected to compete in 64-bit market for several years
notes: Correct prediction: 64-bit Windows arrived with Windows XP x64 in 2005

metric_value: Premier client/server environment: Intel for commercial desktop/low-end servers; Alpha for high-performance technical and commercial desktops and application servers
notes: Clear two-platform strategy: Intel volume, Alpha performance

metric_value: No public strategic commitment to NT; OS/2 division actively competes with NT; ISSC division markets NT — conflicted strategy
notes: IBM's internal conflict between OS/2 and NT divisions was real and well documented

metric_value: Stated NT leadership commitment but protecting HP/UX Unix base; no plans for NT on PA-RISC; will await HP/Intel combined chip for enterprise NT
notes: HP/Intel Itanium chip did come in 2001 as predicted — but too late and with limited NT success

metric_value: Deploys NT clients; no strategic NT server commitment; Ultra positioned to directly compete with NT servers
notes: Sun's Java strategy (announced 1995) was beginning to emerge as alternative to NT for server side

metric_value: Meets product breadth/depth requirements but lacks enterprise-level systems integration and deployment experience necessary for mission-critical NT
notes: Aberdeen's gap assessment proved ironic: Compaq addressed this by acquiring DEC 18 months later

metric_value: Binary translation/emulation enables 32-bit Windows/NT x86 apps on Alpha with significant performance improvements over x86 architectures
notes: FX!32 was genuinely innovative; made Intel x86 apps run faster on Alpha than on native x86

metric_value: Three-tier: ClientWORKS (desktop) -> ServerWORKS Manager (workgroup SNMP) -> POLYCENTER/CA Unicenter (enterprise)
notes: Alliance for Enterprise Management with CA provided heterogeneous enterprise management capability

metric_value: Aug 1995 alliance: Digital technology swap for Microsoft underwriting NT/Office/BackOffice training for thousands of Digital consultants; Digital has 1,000 MCPs — largest MS professional services org in world
notes: Microsoft needed enterprise deployment partner; Digital needed product training funding — genuine strategic alignment

metric_value: Microsoft to work with Digital on 64-bit NT addressing for next major post-4.0 NT release
notes: 64-bit Windows eventually arrived as Windows XP x64 (2005) and Windows Server 2003 x64, but not directly from this partnership

metric_value: Dual-channel: direct sales to create demand, indirect (distributors/VARs/SIs) for order fulfillment; internal sales force compensated for VAR referrals eliminating channel conflict
source_page: The Key to Digital's NT Success — Channels
notes: No-channel-conflict model was innovative; internal staff earned commissions for VAR referrals

metric_name: Digital NT enterprise market leadership prediction
metric_value: Digital is clearly in an NT leadership position today — this race is Digital's to win (or lose); Aberdeen sees positive indications that Digital can execute strategy successfully
notes: Aberdeen was highly bullish on Digital's NT enterprise prospects; parenthetical (or lose) acknowledges execution risk

metric_name: Digital NT enterprise market leadership — actual outcome
metric_value: Digital acquired by Compaq January 1998 for $9.6B; Digital's NT product line and expertise absorbed into Compaq; independent NT market leadership not achieved
notes: Compaq acquired DEC less than 18 months after this profile was published; Compaq then acquired by HP in 2002

metric_value: With Digital's enterprise services and Microsoft's NT, NT will become the dominant enterprise server platform; days of Digital's rapid growth will return
notes: The NT dominance prediction was correct; Digital benefiting was not

metric_value: Windows NT and Windows 2000 Server did become dominant enterprise server platform by 2000-2002; Unix market share declined significantly; NT adoption prediction was accurate
notes: NT/Windows 2000 Server dominated enterprise server market as predicted; Linux also emerged as unexpected alternative

metric_value: IBM's conflicted OS/2 vs NT strategy and lack of public NT commitment leaves door wide open for Digital in enterprise NT
notes: Aberdeen correctly assessed IBM's strategic conflict on NT

metric_name: IBM NT competitive posture — actual outcome
metric_value: OS/2 was discontinued in 2001; IBM never achieved strategic NT server commitment; IBM shifted focus to Linux (2000) instead; prediction of IBM weakness in enterprise NT was accurate
notes: IBM's OS/2 market share collapsed; IBM made Linux its server strategy rather than NT

metric_value: NT clustering capabilities released for all Intel Prioris servers and AlphaServers 1000/2000/4000; NT RAID storage options added for high-availability
notes: Digital released NT clustering before Microsoft's Wolfpack; VAX cluster heritage applied to NT

metric_value: AlphaServer 1000/2000 for workgroup file/print/groupware; 2100/4000/4100 for large department power and performance; all NT cluster-capable
notes: Full Alpha server range from workgroup to enterprise covered mission-critical deployment requirements

metric_value: Digital is quite likely the only systems supplier capable of addressing all four basic buying criteria for mission-critical NT deployment
notes: Strong claim proven accurate in the short term but moot after acquisition; Compaq and HP later addressed these criteria

metric_value: 2,000+ NT applications on Intel Celebris XL Personal Workstation; 1,200+ on Alpha-based Alpha XL
notes: From personal workstation study context — consistent with enterprise NT application depth claims
